abstraction_leaks.description: Controllers exposing database-specific concepts and implementation details

locations.leak: Direct use of MongoDB aggregation pipelines
locations.impact: Controllers must know about MongoDB aggregation syntax and cannot easily switch databases

locations.leak: Direct use of distinct() and countDocuments() methods
locations.code: Tracking.distinct('recipientId', { campaignId, type: 'open' }); Tracking.countDocuments({ campaignId, type: 'open' });
locations.impact: Business logic depends on specific MongoDB query methods

locations.leak: Direct use of Mongoose validation and ObjectId casting
locations.code: Domain.findOne({ _id: id, userId: req.user.id }); // and similar
locations.impact: Controllers tightly coupled to Mongoose ODM

locations.leak: Static methods exposing database-specific findOne and save operations
locations.code: reserveSlot: async function () { const config = await this.findOne({ singleton: 'global' }); ... await config.save(); }
locations.impact: Service layer would need to know about persistence implementation

abstraction_leaks.description: APIs exposing implementation details of underlying services

locations.leak: BullMQ-specific job configuration exposed through API behavior
locations.impact: Clients may depend on specific job queue implementation details

locations.leak: Postfix-specific event structure exposed through SSE
locations.code: parser.injectEvent({ status: 'sent', recipient: jobRecipient.email, domain: sendingDomain.domainName, ... });
locations.impact: Frontend couples to Postfix-specific event fields

locations.code: res.json({ message: 'Tracking event deleted successfully', id }); // where id is MongoDB _id
locations.impact: Frontend couples to MongoDB ObjectId representation

abstraction_leaks.description: Abstractions leaking implementation details through their interfaces

locations.leak: Exposing internal buffer and stats through getter methods
locations.impact: Consumers depend on internal data structures rather than logical events

locations.leak: Exposing internal PHASES constant through getPhaseLabel method
locations.code: getPhaseLabel() { if (!this.enabled) return 'Manual'; return this.getPhase().label; } // where getPhase() returns element from PHASES
locations.impact: Consumers know about the phase label structure

locations.leak: Exposing internal transports array through size getter
locations.impact: Consumers know about internal pool implementation

abstraction_leaks.description: Configuration details leaking into inappropriate layers

locations.leak: Hard-coded Docker container name in postfix log parser
locations.impact: Tightly coupled to specific deployment infrastructure

locations.leak: Hard-coded DNS servers in email verifier
locations.impact: Tightly coupled to specific DNS providers
Tightly coupled to specific selector preferences

abstraction_leaks.summary: Abstraction leaks are relatively mild in this codebase, with most occurring in database-specific code in controllers and configuration details in utilities. The core business logic is reasonably well encapsulated, but there are opportunities to improve abstraction boundaries.
